title: RAG Fundamentals
description: Retrieval pipelines, chunking, reranking, grounding, citations, and RAG evaluation.

questions.title: When would you use RAG instead of fine-tuning?
questions.scenario: You are building an assistant over HR policies, product docs, and customer contracts that change weekly.
questions.expectedAnswer: Use RAG when answers depend on external, changing, proprietary, or access-controlled knowledge. RAG retrieves relevant evidence at query time and grounds the answer. Fine-tuning is better for changing model behavior, style, or task performance, but it is not ideal for frequently changing facts. Then anchor the answer in the scenario by describing where the idea is applied, what decision it supports, which tradeoffs matter, and how you would validate the outcome.

answerFramework.scenarioResponse: Start from the scenario: You are building an assistant over HR policies, product docs, and customer contracts that change weekly. In the interview, answer by naming the problem, stating the decision you would make, and explaining why that decision fits the business and technical constraints.
answerFramework.howToApply: Apply it by mapping the concept to a concrete workflow, choosing the simplest suitable technique, validating it with data or user feedback, and explaining how it changes the product or platform behavior.
answerFramework.example: Example: when asked "When would you use RAG instead of fine-tuning?", use the scenario to show where the idea applies, what you would do first, what result you expect, and how you would know the decision worked in a real rag fundamentals setting.
answerFramework.tradeoffs: Mention tradeoffs explicitly: quality versus cost, latency versus completeness, autonomy versus control, simplicity versus flexibility, and where human review or governance is needed. Then close with how you would measure success.

questions.title: Walk through a basic RAG pipeline.
questions.scenario: You need to explain the architecture to a team building its first document assistant.
questions.expectedAnswer: A basic RAG pipeline ingests documents, parses and cleans content, chunks text, enriches metadata, creates embeddings, indexes vectors, retrieves candidates for a query, optionally reranks them, assembles prompt context, generates an answer, cites sources, and logs traces for evaluation. A strong answer separates offline ingestion from online query flow. Then anchor the answer in the scenario by describing where the idea is applied, what decision it supports, which tradeoffs matter, and how you would validate the outcome.

answerFramework.scenarioResponse: Start from the scenario: You need to explain the architecture to a team building its first document assistant. questions.title: How do you choose a chunking strategy for RAG?
questions.scenario: A document intelligence system must answer questions across PDFs, contracts, and long policy documents.
questions.expectedAnswer: Chunking should preserve semantic meaning while keeping chunks small enough for precise retrieval. Strategy depends on document structure, query patterns, model context, and citation needs. Options include fixed-size chunks, semantic chunks, section-aware chunks, parent-child retrieval, and overlap. Then anchor the answer in the scenario by describing where the idea is applied, what decision it supports, which tradeoffs matter, and how you would validate the outcome.

questions.title: Why is reranking useful in RAG?
questions.scenario: A vector search returns many loosely related chunks and answer quality is inconsistent.
questions.expectedAnswer: First-stage retrieval optimizes recall and speed. Reranking uses a more precise model, often a cross-encoder or LLM-based scorer, to reorder candidate passages by relevance. This improves context precision and reduces prompt pollution, at the cost of additional latency and compute. Then anchor the answer in the scenario by describing where the idea is applied, what decision it supports, which tradeoffs matter, and how you would validate the outcome.

questions.title: How would you evaluate a RAG application?
questions.scenario: Leadership wants proof that an enterprise assistant is accurate enough for internal rollout.
questions.expectedAnswer: Evaluate retrieval and generation separately. Retrieval metrics include context recall, context precision, hit rate, and MRR. Generation metrics include groundedness, faithfulness, answer relevance, citation accuracy, refusal quality, and human preference. Use golden datasets, adversarial tests, regression tests, observability, and feedback loops. Then anchor the answer in the scenario by describing where the idea is applied, what decision it supports, which tradeoffs matter, and how you would validate the outcome.

answerFramework.scenarioResponse: Start from the scenario: Leadership wants proof that an enterprise assistant is accurate enough for internal rollout.
